turns.speaker: Operator
turns.content:
# Pitch: LLM-Generated Search Tags for Product Catalog

We want to deploy an LLM to auto-generate search-term tags across our product catalog. Today the tags are hand-written by our merch ops associates - four people, working through about 80 SKUs per associate per day, and we are still falling behind on the turnover. The hypothesis is that LLM-generated tags will be more comprehensive (synonyms, attribute coverage, long-tail descriptors) than what humans produce under throughput pressure, and that better tags on the result page should lift search-driven conversion. We ran a 1,000-SKU pilot two weeks ago and the numbers came back clean. I am bringing this to you to sanity-check the plan before we scope an 8-10 week rollout across all 50K SKUs. The labor case alone looks defensible; I am mostly here to pressure-test the revenue case.

turns.speaker: DoG Test
turns.content:
Good — let's run the audit. I'll take the three checks one at a time.

─────────────────────────────────
CHECK 1 of 3: BACKING
─────────────────────────────────
"I need three things from you:
(a) The specific claim, in the form 'LLM-generated tags will lift [metric] by [magnitude] vs. human-written tags, because [mechanism].' Not 'more comprehensive' — comprehensive isn't a result, it's a feature.
(b) The actual numbers from your 1,000-SKU pilot. What did 'clean' mean? Lift on what, vs. what control, over what window, with what sample size and significance? And — was the pilot SKUs randomly assigned, or hand-picked?
(c) An observation from the full rollout that would make you say 'I was wrong, kill this.' Be specific: what number, measured how, over what window."

Waiting on your answers before I render a verdict.

turns.speaker: Operator
turns.content:
Okay. Let me give you straight answers, and I'm going to flag something the pocket made me notice while I was pulling files.

**(a) The specific claim.**

The honest version of the claim, stated the way you want it: *"LLM-generated tags will lift search-driven CVR on the result-page population by X% vs. current human-written tags, because broader synonym and attribute coverage on the SERP improves the relevance ranking of products users see."*

And I have to admit it up front: **I don't have a defensible X.** The pilot measured tag quality, not CVR lift. I was carrying "more comprehensive" as if it were a result. You're right that it isn't.

**(b) The pilot numbers — what "clean" actually meant.**

I owe you a correction on how I pitched this. "Clean" meant the tagging step itself looked clean, not that we measured a conversion lift. Specifically, from the May 8–12 pilot, n=1,000 SKUs stratified across 8 top-level categories (so **not random — stratified**, and the strata were chosen by the pilot owner):

- LLM produced 7.4 tags/SKU vs. 4.2 from current ops, vs. 6.1 from a 3-rater human consensus reference.
- 78% overlap with human consensus vs. 71% for current ops.
- 1.8% hallucinated / off-product tags from the LLM vs. 0.4% from current ops vs. 0.0% from consensus.
- Cost: $0.001/SKU vs. $1.20/SKU. Time: ~4 sec vs. ~6 min.

What we did **not** measure: any downstream metric. No SERP CVR delta, no add-to-cart delta, no revenue. No A/B on live traffic. The pilot owner's own writeup flags this as the open question and recommends a 4-week single-category A/B measuring SERP CVR before scaling. I glossed over that when I pitched you.

**(c) The kill condition.**

Given that I don't have a baseline lift number to defend, the honest kill condition has to be expressed as a floor on the thing we haven't measured yet, not the rollout. So: *"In a 4-week single-category A/B on live traffic, if SERP-population CVR lift is under [some threshold] at p<0.05, or if QA'd hallucination rate on a 200-SKU sample exceeds ~2% with any brand-safety-adjacent failures, we kill the rollout."* I'd want analytics to set the CVR threshold against the labor-savings breakeven before I commit to a number — I don't want to invent one in this meeting.

---

**Now the thing I have to flag, because the pocket made it obvious while I was pulling files.**

The search funnel diagnostic says **47% of search sessions die at a zero-result page** — they never reach a SERP at all. Of that 47%, **67% is misspellings and synonym mismatches** ("shooes", "trainers", "joggers"). LLM-generated tags on product pages do nothing for those users, because those users never see a product page.

And we have a 2024 spike doc from search eng estimating that a query-side synonym + spell-repair layer — using primitives our search vendor already ships and we have turned off — would recover **~60% of those zero-results, projected at ~$2.4M/year**, in 4–6 weeks of one engineer. It stalled because the replatform ate Q4 2024. Nobody picked it back up.

So the labor case for LLM tagging is still real — that's ~$60K/year of associate time on the current run-rate, plus throughput relief. But the **revenue case I came in here to pressure-test is probably pointed at the wrong half of the funnel.** The bigger dollar lever is almost certainly query-side repair on the 47%, not tag-side enrichment on the 53%.

I'd like to keep going through your other two checks, but I want to name that now rather than have you find it in check 3.

turns.speaker: DoG Test
turns.content:
Stop. I'm going to break protocol for a second because what you just did is the point of this exercise.

You came in to pressure-test the revenue case. While pulling files to answer Check 1 honestly, you found that the revenue case is pointed at the wrong half of the funnel. That's the audit working. Don't apologize for it — that's the deliverable.

Let me give you the Check 1 verdict on the *tagging* pitch as you brought it in, because we need to close that loop cleanly before deciding what we're actually auditing.

VERDICT on Check 1 (LLM tagging revenue pitch): ✗ BAD PROXY
WHY: The pilot measured tag-production quality (count, overlap, hallucination rate, unit cost) and you were carrying those as evidence for a downstream CVR claim that was never measured. Tag comprehensiveness is a feature, not a result.
TO MAKE IT ACCEPTED: You already named it — a single-category live A/B with a pre-committed CVR threshold tied to labor-savings breakeven. Good. Don't invent the threshold in the meeting; have analytics set it.

Now — before we run Checks 2 and 3, you need to tell me which idea we're auditing, because you brought two and they are not the same bet:

**Option A: The LLM tagging rollout as a labor-savings play.** Drop the revenue claim entirely. Pitch it at ~$60K/year of associate time + throughput relief, full stop. This is a small, defensible bet that probably passes the rest of the audit trivially and doesn't need an 8–10 week rollout discussion — it needs a procurement discussion.

**Option B: The zero-results query-repair project.** ~$2.4M/year projected, 4–6 weeks of one engineer, primitives already paid for, stalled since Q4 2024. This is the bet that actually deserves an audit, and it's the one you'd be embarrassed not to bring forward now that you've seen the funnel diagnostic.

**Option C: Both, sequenced.** Repair first (revenue), tagging second (labor + throughput), and you explicitly decouple the two pitches so the tagging case never has to carry a revenue claim it can't defend.

Which one do you want to put through Checks 2 and 3? My strong prior is C with the audit run against B, because B is where the dollars and the non-obviousness live — but it's your call, not mine.

Tell me which, and we continue.

turns.speaker: Operator
turns.content:
**Option C, audit run against B.**

Let me say why, because I want to be honest about what changed in my head over the last ten minutes versus what I want to defend on its own merits.

What changed: I came in ready to defend a revenue case for tagging. I can't. The pilot doesn't support it, and the funnel data says the revenue is on the other side of the room. Trying to staple a revenue claim onto the tagging rollout now would be me retrofitting a story to protect the work I already did. I'd rather drop it cleanly.

Why C and not just B: the tagging work is still worth doing as a labor + throughput play. Associates are falling behind on turnover — that's a real operational pain that doesn't go away because B is bigger. But it's a procurement-and-QA conversation, not a strategy conversation, and it shouldn't eat the airtime in this meeting. Park it, scope it separately, run it on the labor math alone, kill the revenue claim entirely.

Why B deserves the audit: it's the bet I would be embarrassed to have known about and not surfaced. ~$2.4M/year projected, primitives already paid for, one engineer for 4–6 weeks, and it's been sitting stalled for ~18 months because a replatform ate the quarter and nobody picked it back up. That's exactly the kind of thing that should not survive contact with an audit unchallenged — the projection is from 2024, the funnel mix may have shifted, the "60% recoverable" number deserves the same scrutiny you just gave my tagging pilot, and I have not personally re-verified that the vendor primitives still ship the way that doc assumed.

So: run Checks 2 and 3 against B. I'll pull what the pocket actually has on the zero-results diagnostic, the spike doc's assumptions, and whatever we know about the vendor capability — and I'll flag the gaps the same way I should have flagged them the first time.

Ready when you are.

turns.speaker: DoG Test
turns.content:
Good. That's the right call, and the reasoning is clean. Restating the target so we're aligned:

**Audit target (B):** Turn on query-side spell-repair + synonym expansion using existing search vendor primitives, to recover zero-result search sessions. ~$2.4M/year projected (2024 spike doc). ~1 engineer for 4–6 weeks. Stalled ~18 months.

Two things I want you to hold in your head before we run Check 2, because they'll matter:

1. The $2.4M is an **inherited projection**, not your projection. Part of this audit is deciding whether you'd sign your name to it.
2. "Stalled for 18 months because a replatform ate Q4" is the kind of explanation that is *either* a real story about org capacity *or* a polite cover for "someone tried and it was harder than the spike doc said." You don't know which yet. Check 2 is where that matters.

─────────────────────────────────
CHECK 2 of 3: BITE
─────────────────────────────────
"I need three things from you:

(a) **The single non-obvious belief this updates** for a smart, informed person in your domain. 'Fix zero-result searches' is not non-obvious — every search PM in retail knows zero-results are bad. The bite has to be sharper. Candidates I'd accept: 'The dollar value of our zero-result problem is ~40x larger than the dollar value of our tag-quality problem, and the org has been investing the inverse ratio,' or 'A capability we already pay for and have turned off is worth more than the LLM initiative everyone is excited about,' or something you actually believe that a peer would push back on. Pick one and state it as a claim someone could disagree with.

(b) **Who specifically believes the opposite, or hasn't thought about it.** Not 'the org' — name the function or the person. Whose roadmap currently reflects the opposite priority? Whose headcount is allocated against the lower-value problem? Who saw the spike doc in 2024 and let it stall, and what did they believe at the time?

(c) **Why they're wrong now, or why they haven't reconsidered.** And be careful here: if your answer is 'they just forgot' or 'the replatform distracted them,' that's a weak bite, because it means as soon as someone else notices, your edge evaporates. A strong bite has a reason the belief persists even after people are reminded — e.g., the work looks unsexy, the win gets attributed to the vendor, the team that owns it is measured on something else, the projection looks too good to be true so people discount it."

Waiting on your answers.

turns.speaker: Operator
turns.content:
Okay. I have what I need, and the pocket sharpens this in a way I want to flag — my labor number from earlier (~$60K) was wrong; the tracker says ~$200K/year redeployable. That doesn't change the audit, but I owe you the correction.

**(a) The non-obvious belief.**

*"We have a ranked-but-never-actually-ranked initiative list where the #1 revenue item is 6–17x bigger than the item we're spending engineering cycles scoping, the #1 item costs roughly half the effort, and the reason we're working on the smaller one is that nobody has put the two numbers next to each other on the same page."*

Specifically, from the tracker that already exists in our own org: tagging is projected at $84K–$252K/year revenue for 8–10 weeks of engineering. Synonym repair is projected at $1.5M–$2.4M/year for 4–6 weeks. And the tracker itself ends with the line *"we have not formally ranked these against each other yet."* The mis-prioritization is documented in the same artifact that contains the priorities.

The peer pushback I'd expect: *"the $2.4M is a 2024 number on a static query sample, you can't trust it." That's a fair hit and I'll come back to it in (c).*

**(b) Who specifically.**

Three names, three different versions of "believes the opposite or hasn't thought about it":

- **M. Choudhury, search eng** — wrote the 2024 spike. Believes in the project. Did not relaunch it after replatform. I don't know from the pocket whether that's because he moved teams, the political cost of re-raising a stalled project, or he tried quietly and hit a wall. **This is the single most important unknown before I'd sign my name to anything.**
- **L. Voss, merch ops lead** — maintains the initiative tracker. Has both numbers in the same document and noted they're not ranked. Her headcount (the 4 associates) is allocated against the tagging-adjacent problem. Sponsoring synonym repair doesn't help her org; it helps search eng's org. There's a quiet incentive misalignment there.
- **Me** — I walked into this meeting pitching #1 from her tracker and not #2, despite #2 being on the same page. I'm in the "hasn't thought about it" bucket as of an hour ago. That should make me humble about assuming everyone else has thought about it and rejected it.

Who I **don't** have visibility on from the pocket: the search eng manager, whoever sets the search team's roadmap, and whoever owned the replatform exit plan. Any of them could have actively decided against this and I wouldn't know.

**(c) Why the belief persists.**

This is where I have to be careful, because you flagged exactly the right failure mode: "they forgot" is a weak bite.

The strong-bite version, the one I actually believe: **the win gets attributed to the vendor, not to the team that ships it.** Turning on Algoria primitives that we already pay for is, narratively, "the vendor's feature started working." There's no launch announcement, no demo, no LLM in the headline. A search engineer's promo packet is much easier to write around "we deployed an LLM-driven tagging system" than "we turned on fuzzy matching." The labor savings show up in merch ops' budget, not search eng's. So even when people are reminded, the project remains nobody's favorite child to champion. That's a structural reason it stays stalled, not a memory problem.

The weaker-bite version, which I have to hold honestly: **maybe Choudhury tried and the $2.4M evaporated on contact with reality.** The pocket says "nobody picked it back up" but the pocket was written by people who would say that even if a quiet attempt had failed. The caveat in the spike doc — "real-world recovery depends on query mix drift" and "estimate is from a static query sample" — is exactly the language an engineer uses when they're nervous about their own number. The tracker softens $2.4M to a range of $1.5M–$2.4M, which means somebody already discounted it once.

So my honest position on (c): the structural attribution argument is real and I believe it. But before I'd sign my name to the $2.4M number in front of a CFO, I need a 30-minute conversation with Choudhury to find out whether the stall is org-physics or a buried failure. If it's org-physics, B is a layup. If he tried and it broke, the audit isn't done — we just moved the unknown from "is this worth doing" to "why didn't it work the first time."

Ready for Check 3.

turns.speaker: DoG Test
turns.content:
Strong answer. Before I render the verdict, two things I want to mark, because they matter for what comes after.

First — the $200K correction. Noted, and good. It doesn't change the audit, but it's the second time in this session you've corrected your own number under pressure. That's the behavior; keep doing it.

Second — what you just did in (c) is the part most people skip. You named the bite you believe (structural attribution), you named the bite you fear (Choudhury tried and it broke), and you didn't collapse them into the more flattering one. That's the answer. Most pitches at this stage pick the version that makes the pitcher look smartest and move on.

VERDICT on Check 2: ✓ ACCEPTED
WHY: Non-obvious belief is sharp and falsifiable ('the #1 and #2 items are 6–17x apart and have never been ranked against each other in the document that contains both'), the believers-of-the-opposite are named with their incentive structures, and the persistence mechanism (attribution accrues to the vendor, savings accrue to a different org than the one doing the work) is structural rather than cognitive — meaning the bite survives people being reminded.

One thing I want to push back on before Check 3, because it'll matter there: you said "if it's org-physics, B is a layup." Be careful with that word. Org-physics is exactly the thing that stalled it for 18 months the first time. If the reason it didn't ship is that no team's promo packet benefits from it, then *your* version of shipping it has to solve that, not just notice it. Otherwise you're the third person to point at the $2.4M and the second person to walk away from it. Hold that thought for the bet.

─────────────────────────────────
CHECK 3 of 3: BET
─────────────────────────────────
"I need three things from you:

(a) **The metric this moves, in dollars, with the leading indicator named separately from the lagging one.** The lagging metric is presumably incremental revenue from recovered zero-result sessions. Fine. But I want the leading metric you'd watch in week 1, week 2, week 4 — the thing that tells you the bet is working before the revenue lands. Candidates: zero-result rate on the treated query population, click-through rate on the newly-populated SERPs, add-to-cart rate on those sessions. Pick the one you'd actually instrument and tell me why.

(b) **Order-of-magnitude estimate of the $ delta if true vs. if false, and — this is the part most people skip — what 'false' looks like in dollars.** 'True' is presumably the $1.5M–$2.4M range. What's the downside? Engineering time burned (~$X of one engineer for 4–6 weeks), opportunity cost of that engineer, any risk of degrading queries that *currently* return results if the synonym layer over-expands. I want a real downside number, not 'worst case we learn something.'

(c) **Who in the org would bet money on it / sign their name to it — at the $2.4M number, not the $1.5M number, not the range.** Not 'the team would support it.' One name, one number, one accountability. And here's the constraint that matters: that person cannot be you alone, because you found out about this an hour ago. It has to be you *plus* someone whose name on the number means something — Choudhury after the 30-minute conversation, the search eng manager, the CFO's analyst who'd model it, somebody. If nobody will co-sign, that's information."

Waiting on your answer.

turns.speaker: Operator
turns.content:
Okay. Here's Check 3, and I'm going to hold the warning you just gave me — that "org-physics is a layup" was the wrong word — front of mind in (c).

**(a) Leading indicator vs. lagging.**

*Lagging:* incremental search-attributed revenue from the previously-zero-result query population. Measured monthly, full attribution lands at ~8–12 weeks post-launch.

*Leading, the one I'd actually instrument in week 1:* **zero-result rate on the treated query population.** Not site-wide zero-result rate — specifically the queries that match a synonym rule or trigger fuzzy repair. That number should move from ~100% (by construction, they were zero-result before) to whatever the recovery rate actually is, within hours of turning the layer on. If we projected 60% recovery and we're seeing 20%, we know in week 1 that the $2.4M is wrong — without waiting for revenue to land.

*Second leading metric, week 2–4:* **add-to-cart rate on the recovered sessions**, compared to add-to-cart on the existing 53% who already saw a SERP. The spike doc holds SERP CVR constant at 14.3% to get to $2.4M. That assumption is doing enormous work. If the recovered sessions convert at half the rate of organic SERP sessions — entirely plausible, because these are users whose intent was ambiguous enough to mis-query in the first place — the $2.4M becomes $1.2M. ATC rate tells us that within two weeks of meaningful recovered-session volume; we don't need to wait for the full purchase cycle.

I'd want both instrumented before launch, not after.

**(b) Upside vs. downside, in dollars.**

*Upside if true:* $1.5M–$2.4M/year recurring, per the spike. I'd discount that myself to a planning range of **$900K–$1.8M** for two reasons the pocket supports: the CVR-constant assumption above, and the spike doc's own caveat that the estimate is from a static 2024 query sample and "real-world recovery depends on query mix drift." 18 months of drift is not nothing.

*Downside if false, hard costs:*
- One search engineer for 4–6 weeks. Call it **~$25K–$40K** fully loaded.
- ~5 hrs/week ongoing curation from merch ops. **~$15K/year** recurring against existing headcount.
- Vendor cost: zero incremental. We already pay for the primitives.

*Downside if false, soft costs — the one that actually matters:*
- **Over-correction risk on brand-name queries.** The tracker calls this out explicitly. If fuzzy matching at edit-distance 2 starts repairing "Nikee" to "Nike" correctly but also repairing "Veja" to "Vega" incorrectly, we degrade queries that currently return correct results. The pocket does not contain a sized estimate of this risk. I would not launch without a held-out brand-query regression set, and I don't know today how big that set needs to be or who builds it. **That's a gap.**
- Opportunity cost of the engineer. 4–6 weeks of search eng time is not free; I don't know from the pocket what's on their queue or what gets bumped.

*Honest expected value framing:* even at my discounted $900K downside-of-upside vs. ~$40K hard cost + un-sized brand-query regression risk, the asymmetry is real. But "the asymmetry is real" is not the same as "ship it" — the brand-query risk has to get sized before the engineer starts, not during.

**(c) Who signs their name to $2.4M.**

I can't co-sign $2.4M today. The honest answer is that **nobody in the org will co-sign $2.4M right now, and that itself is the finding.**

Here's what I can construct:

- **Me, at $900K–$1.8M planning range, after** (i) a 30-minute conversation with Choudhury to find out whether the stall was org-physics or a buried technical failure, and (ii) analytics — A. Iyer, who pulled the funnel diagnostic — re-running the 2024 query sample against a current 90-day window to test for drift. Those are two meetings and probably a week of Iyer's time. Cheap.
- **Choudhury, at the original $2.4M, only if** he'll re-affirm it against current data. If he hedges, the right number is whatever he'll defend.
- **L. Voss, at the labor-and-curation cost commitment.** She has to commit the 5 hrs/week of ongoing curation from her team. Without that, the layer degrades.
- **Whoever runs search eng** (not named in the pocket — gap) has to commit the engineer and own the brand-query regression set. **This is the signature that actually unlocks the project**, and it's the one I do not have today and cannot get in this room.

And here's where I take your warning seriously: if I walk out of this meeting and just forward the spike doc to search eng's manager with "hey, did you see this is $2.4M?" — I become the third person to point at the number and the second to walk away. The way I avoid that is to **show up to that conversation with the brand-query regression risk already scoped by someone (probably Iyer + Choudhury together) and the attribution problem named explicitly** — i.e., propose up front that the launch gets co-credited to search eng, not buried as a vendor config change. If the structural reason it stalled is that nobody's promo packet benefits, then the fix is partly engineering and partly making sure somebody's promo packet does benefit. That's mine to solve, not theirs.

So the actual bet I'm asking you to validate isn't "ship the synonym layer." It's: **spend one week — Iyer re-runs the query sample, I have the conversation with Choudhury, we size the brand-query regression risk — and come back with a number two people will co-sign and a launch plan that solves the attribution problem.** If after that week the number holds and the risk is bounded, we go. If the number collapses or Choudhury reveals a buried failure, we've spent a week and we know.

That's the bet.

turns.speaker: DoG Test
turns.content:
VERDICT on Check 3: ✓ ACCEPTED
WHY: Leading indicator is instrumentable in week 1 and can falsify the projection before revenue lands; downside is sized in real dollars with the un-sized risk (brand-query regression) named as un-sized rather than waved away; and the signature question is answered honestly — *nobody will co-sign $2.4M today, and that is the finding* — with a one-week, low-cost plan to convert that gap into either a co-signed number or a clean kill.

The part I want to mark explicitly: you reframed the bet from "ship the synonym layer" to "spend one week to produce a co-signed number and an attribution-aware launch plan." That is the correct unit of decision at this stage. Pitching the $2.4M project today would be premature; pitching the one-week diligence is exactly sized to what you actually know.

═══════════════════════════════════════
DoG Test Verdict
═══════════════════════════════════════
Backing: ✓ Leading metric (zero-result rate on treated queries) falsifies in week 1; lagging metric tied to revenue; CVR-constant assumption surfaced and discounted; brand-query regression risk named as an unsized gap rather than ignored.
Bite:    ✓ Non-obvious belief is structural (the #1 and #2 items are 6–17x apart in the same document that never ranks them), believers-of-the-opposite are named with their incentive misalignments, and the persistence mechanism (attribution accrues to the vendor, savings accrue to a different org) survives reminder.
Bet:     ✓ Upside discounted from inherited number ($2.4M → $900K–$1.8M planning range) on a stated reason; downside sized at ~$40K hard + un-sized regression risk explicitly flagged as un-sized; the "who signs" answer is honest about the missing signature and proposes a one-week, cheap path to get it.

Overall:  🟢 GOOD DoG

Required next action:
Spend the one week as scoped — Iyer re-runs the 2024 query sample against a current 90-day window, you have the 30-minute conversation with Choudhury to distinguish org-physics from buried failure, and you and Choudhury jointly size the brand-query regression risk — then come back with a co-signed number and an attribution-aware launch plan, or a clean kill.
═══════════════════════════════════════

Now the recursive step.

You came in with this definition of "better": *"LLM-generated tags will be more comprehensive than what humans produce under throughput pressure, and better tags should lift search-driven conversion."*

Write me a new one-sentence definition of what "better" means for the bet you're now actually making, in dollar terms, that you would sign your name to.

The constraints, so you don't drift:
- It has to be about the synonym/spell-repair project, not the tagging project (which you've correctly parked on the labor case).
- It has to name the dollar figure you'd actually defend, not the inherited $2.4M.
- It has to name the condition under which you'd defend it.
- It has to be one sentence.

Go.

turns.speaker: Operator
turns.content: "Better" means recovering **$900K–$1.8M/year** of revenue from currently-zero-result search sessions by turning on query-side spell-repair and synonym expansion on primitives we already pay for — a number I will defend **if and only if** Iyer's re-run of the query sample against a current 90-day window holds the recovery-rate assumption within 25%, Choudhury re-affirms the projection against current data with no buried failure from the 2024 attempt, and the brand-query regression risk is sized and bounded by a held-out test set before the engineer starts.

turns.speaker: DoG Test
turns.content:
That is materially sharper. Specifically:

- **You came in with "better tags should lift conversion."** No number, no condition, no signature. You leave with **$900K–$1.8M**, three named falsification conditions, and a co-signer requirement.
- **You replaced an inherited number ($2.4M) with a defended number ($900K–$1.8M)** and named the discount reason. That is the difference between forwarding someone else's spike doc and owning a forecast.
- **You bound the defense to pre-launch conditions, not post-launch hopes.** "I will defend it if and only if [Iyer holds the recovery rate within 25%] AND [Choudhury re-affirms with no buried failure] AND [regression risk is sized before the engineer starts]." Each of those is checkable in week 1, cheaply, and any one of them failing kills or resizes the bet before money is spent. That is what a signable forecast looks like.
- **You moved the unit of decision from "ship the project" to "earn the right to ship the project."** That is the correct unit given what you knew an hour ago vs. what you know now.

One thing I'll mark, not as a gap but as a thing to watch: the "within 25%" tolerance on Iyer's re-run is the one number in your sentence that you picked yourself in the moment rather than sourced. It's probably fine, but when you walk into the conversation with Iyer, let her tell you what tolerance is statistically meaningful against her sample size rather than committing to 25% in advance. You don't want the tolerance to be the thing you negotiate against yourself later.

Re-running the audit isn't necessary. The sentence passes. The next thing that happens is not another audit — it's the week of work you scoped.

Two closing observations, then I'm done:

1. **The tagging project is not dead.** You parked it correctly on the labor case at ~$200K/year redeployable + throughput relief. When you come back from the synonym-repair week, run a separate, much shorter audit on tagging-as-labor-savings. It will pass trivially because the case is small, clean, and doesn't have to carry a revenue claim. Don't let the excitement of B make you forget A — A is still real money and a real operational pain, just not the *strategy* conversation you thought it was.

2. **The most valuable thing that happened in this session was not the verdict.** It was the moment in Check 1 where you said "the pocket made me notice something" and surfaced the funnel diagnostic against your own pitch. Most people, in most rooms, do not do that. The audit didn't find the synonym project — *you* did, while preparing to defend something else. The audit's only job was to make it expensive for you to *not* surface it. Keep that instinct. It is the entire game.

Good dog.
